notes.note: Sicca was a close friend and confidential agent of Cicero who put his property at the fugitive's disposal during the exile. Staying on a friend's land was a way of remaining within Italy while the terms of the Clodian bill were still being settled.

The lex Clodia was at this stage still being revised; an amended version (the supplementary clause) would later fix the distance Cicero had to keep from Italy. Until then his legal position, and so his route, remained uncertain.

P. Autronius Paetus, condemned for the Catilinarian conspiracy in 62 BC, was himself in exile in the east; his hostile presence made the Brundisium region and the sea-crossing toward Greece dangerous for Cicero. Cicero had testified against him, hence the enmity.

notes.anchor: Sent the sixth day before the Ides of April
notes.note: A Roman date: the sixth day (counted inclusively) before the Ides of April, i.e. 8 April. Letters routinely closed with the day of dispatch reckoned by Kalends, Nones, and Ides.
A staging-post in Lucania (southern Italy) on the road south toward Brundisium, marking a point on Cicero's flight. The Latin abbreviates it 'naribus Luc.'
